title: Coleslaw
subtitle: Crisp white cabbage and carrot in a mustardy mayonnaise dressing.
excerpt: White cabbage and carrot shredded thin, tossed in a dressing of mayonnaise, cider vinegar, and Dijon mustard. Salted and rested before dressing to draw out moisture and keep the cabbage crisp. Better than any shop-bought version.
sourceNotes: Coleslaw derives from the Dutch koolsla (cabbage salad), brought to the Americas by Dutch settlers and eventually popularised across the English-speaking world. The mayonnaise-dressed version became the British standard from the mid-twentieth century as commercial mayonnaise became widely available. The salting technique to prevent a watery dressing is the main improvement over the commercial product.

recipe.batchNotes: Keeps in the fridge for three days. Make a large batch for parties and barbecues.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Best made 30 minutes ahead to allow the dressing to absorb, but no more than a day ahead or the cabbage loses crunch.

content.text: Shop-bought coleslaw is watery because the cabbage was never salted before dressing. Salt draws moisture out of the cabbage so it does not dilute the dressing later. Thirty minutes of resting with salt makes the difference.

Slice the cabbage as thinly as you can by hand: a large knife works better than a box grater, which can produce uneven shreds that go soft too quickly. The carrot can be coarsely grated — it does not need to match the cabbage exactly.

items.prepNote: about 600 g, core removed, finely shredded

content.text: A large mixing bowl and a sharp knife. A mandoline produces the finest cabbage shreds but is optional.

Put the shredded cabbage in a large bowl. Sprinkle over {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt, toss well, and leave for 30 minutes. The cabbage will wilt and a pool of water will collect in the bottom of the bowl.

Tip the salted cabbage into a colander and rinse briefly under cold water to remove excess salt. Squeeze in small handfuls to remove as much liquid as possible. Return to the dried bowl with the grated carrot and spring onion if using.

Mix {{mayonnaise}} of mayonnaise, {{dijon-mustard}} of Dijon mustard, {{cider-vinegar}} of cider vinegar, and {{caster-sugar}} of sugar together in a small bowl. Season with {{black-pepper}} of black pepper. Pour the dressing over the cabbage and carrot and toss thoroughly. Leave for at least 10 minutes before serving so the dressing absorbs.

items.symptom: Coleslaw is watery an hour after dressing
items.cause: Cabbage was not salted and dried thoroughly, or rested too long after dressing.
items.fix: Drain off the liquid that collects and toss again. For next time, take more time squeezing the cabbage dry before dressing.

items.cause: Cabbage was not rinsed after salting.
items.fix: The rinsing step removes most of the salt. If too salty, balance with a little more sugar and vinegar.
Aim for 1–2 mm shreds. If the cabbage is still tough, add a little more salt and leave for another 15 minutes to wilt further.
Add a splash more cider vinegar and a teaspoon of water to loosen. Balance with more mustard to keep the sharpness.

content.text: Coleslaw arrived in British supermarkets in its ready-made form in the 1960s and became one of the most commonly eaten side dishes at barbecues, picnics, and alongside sandwiches. The homemade version requires very little effort and is significantly better: the cabbage is crisper, the dressing has more character, and it does not taste of the preservatives that give commercial versions their particular quality. It is the kind of side dish that quietly earns its place at every outdoor meal.
